calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Independent Neuropsychological Medical Examination Of Plaintiff Daniel Owens
Off Calendar May-21-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT RUTH WONG'S MOTION TO COMPEL INDEPENDENT NEUROPSYCHOLOGICAL MEDICAL EXAMINATION OF PLAINTIFF DANIEL OWENS

Appearances via Zoom:
Michael Stephenson of Bay Area Bicycle Law, PC (415.466.8717, M.Stephenson@BayAreaBicycleLaw.com) for plaintiffs and opposing parties;
Max Neiman of Phillips Spallas & Angstadt LLP (415.278.9400) for defendant and movng party Ruth Wong.

Argued and the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Ruth Wong moves for an order compelling an independent neuropsychological medical examination of plaintiff Daniel Owens. The motion is denied without prejudice.

Wong's notice of the examination includes a two-page list of tests that may be administered. The court recognizes that the examiner needs flexibility in conducting the examination, and that some tests that are listed on a notice of examination may not be administered. Here, however, the list of possible examinations is so long that it does not meaningfully limit the examiner's testing choices or enable Owens's counsel to take an informed position about the list of tests. Such a lengthy list of all possible tests that might be administered is tantamount to a notice that lists only the type of tests, which was prohibited in Carpenter v. Superior Court (2006) 141 Cal.App.4th 249, 271.

Because the court anticipates the motion may be renewed after Wong's expert has developed a more realistic and tailored list of tests, it provides further guidance to the parties. By statute, the plaintiff must be permitted to record audio of the examination in its entirety. Code of Civil Procedure, sec. 2032.530, subdivision (a), states, "[t]he examiner and examinee shall have the right to record a mental examination by audio technology." There is no exception in the statute for the testing portion of the examination.

The parties also dispute whether the examiner must turn over the raw data and scores to Owens's counsel. Case law makes clear that "there is no express statutory authority" for the position that plaintiff's counsel "is entitled to copies of the written test materials and [plaintiff's] answers," i.e. the raw test data that Owens seeks. (See Carpenter, supra, 141 Cal.App.4th, at p. 271.) "[N]either is there statutory authority precluding a trial court, in its discretion, from ordering the disclosure of the written test questions and answers." (Id.) A trial court does not abuse its discretion in ordering production of raw data to plaintiff's counsel where defendant's expert, in opposing the release of raw data, "did not explain why a protective order would not ameliorate those dangers . . . [and] did not explain why her ethical obligations would be violated if a court ordered her to disclose the raw data and audio recording to plaintiffs' attorney subject to a protective order." (Randy's Trucking, Inc. v. Superior Court (2023) 91 Cal.App.5th 818, 837-838.) Here, the court finds that Wong's expert has explained the risks of a protective order and the reasons why the expert would recuse if disclosure were ordered. There is a significant risk of trial delay if the court orders disclosure; trial is set for July 2026 and in the court's experience, the recusal of defendant's expert would likely result in a lengthy delay because of the difficulty of retaining experts who are willing to turn over the data Owens seeks. The court concludes here that it is appropriate to order Wong's expert to turn over the raw data and scores to Owens's expert neuropsychologist, conditioned on Owens's expert's agreement not to further disclose the raw data and scores.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Permitting Discovery Of Defendants' Financial Condition
Off Calendar Nov-21-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFFS' Motion For An Order Permitting Discovery Of Defendants' Financial Condition.

Plaintiffs Daniel and Johara Owens's motion for leave to conduct discovery of the financial condition of defendants Ruth Wong, Marilyn Wong, and Garry Wong pursuant to Civil Code, section 3295, subdivision (c), is denied without prejudice to any subsequent order by the trial court once the case is sent out to trial.

Discovery into a defendant's financial condition is subject to Civil Code, section 3295, which prohibits such discovery absent court order. "[B]efore a court may enter an order permitting discovery of a defendant's financial condition, it must (1) weigh the evidence submitted in favor of and in opposition to motion for discovery, and (2) make a finding that it is very likely the plaintiff will prevail on his claim for punitive damages." (Jabro v. Superior Court (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 754, 758.) "Very likely" means more than a reasonable probability, and more than a prima facie showing of the kind needed to defeat summary judgment. (Id.)

The evidence presented here is after-accident photos of the handrail whose defect injured Daniel Owens, steps covered in green mildew, and deposition excerpts of Marilyn Wong, the daughter-in-law of the property owner and a defendant who did not inspect the handrail prior to the accident but had traversed the stairs. The Wongs offer Marilyn Wong's declaration and a counsel declaration attaching further Marilyn Wong deposition excerpts and information about Garry Wong's and Ruth Wong's medical conditions. The court concludes that, weighing the evidence presented on this motion, it does not find that it is very likely that the Owenses will prevail on their claim for punitive damages. The Wongs have presented evidence that Marilyn Wong responded to complaints, inspected the stairs, did not notice the issue with the handrail, and never received any complaints about the stairs. The Owenses' evidence of malice, i.e. despicable conduct carried on with a willful and conscious disregard of the safety of others (Civ. Code, section 3294, subd. (c)(1)), is not so strong as to make it very likely the Owenses will obtain punitive damages.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave Of Court To Amend The Complaint
Off Calendar Apr-16-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFFS DANIEL OWENS, AND JOHARA OWENS' Motion For Leave Of Court To Amend The Complaint.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The motion for leave to file the amended complaint attached as Exhibit 1 to the Smith Declaration is granted. Leave to amend is liberally granted. The court finds no prejudice here and the Smith Declaration establishes that the proposed amendment is based on facts learned in February 2025. Defendant contends that the breach of contract claim is foreclosed by the statute of limitations, but September 2021 is within four years of the present. Plaintiffs shall file the proposed amendment within five court days of entry of this order.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling, with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
